# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains empirical evidence on the scale, structure, readability, maintenance patterns, instruction categories, and automatic classification performance of agent context files such as CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and copilot-instructions.md.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-002
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: metric
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.12884v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The study analyzed 2,303 agent context files from 1,925 repositories.

## Source Excerpt

“We conduct the first large-scale empirical study of 2,303 agent context files from 1,925 repositories.”

## Note

The abstract frames this as the first large-scale empirical study.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-002
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: metric
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Section 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.12884v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The dataset included 922 Claude Code files, 694 OpenAI Codex files, and 687 GitHub Copilot files.

## Source Excerpt

“In total, we collect 922 Claude Code files, 694 OpenAI Codex files, and 687 GitHub Copilot files across 1,925 repositories.”

## Note

These counts are tied to filename-based retrieval using each tool’s documented naming convention.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-002
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: method
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Section 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.12884v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The repository sample started from 8,370 AIDev repositories with at least 5 GitHub stars.

## Source Excerpt

“From this dataset, we select 8,370 repositories with at least 5 GitHub stars to exclude toy projects.”

## Note

This is a sampling constraint on the study corpus, not the full population of agent-using repositories.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-002
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: metric
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Section 4.1.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.12884v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
GitHub Copilot context files had a median length of 535.0 words, Claude Code 485.0 words, and OpenAI Codex 335.5 words.

## Source Excerpt

“Context files for GitHub Copilot (median 535.0 words) and Claude Code (median 485.0 words) are similarly extensive... both are significantly longer than OpenAI Codex context files (median 335.5 words).”

## Note

The source states no statistically significant length difference between GitHub Copilot and Claude Code, but both exceed OpenAI Codex.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-002
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Section 4.1.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.12884v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Median Flesch Reading Ease scores were 16.6 for Claude Code files, 26.6 for GitHub Copilot files, and 39.6 for OpenAI Codex files.

## Source Excerpt

“Claude Code context files are the most difficult to read, with a median FRE score of 16.6... GitHub Copilot context files, while also ‘very difficult’ (median FRE 26.6)... OpenAI Codex context files are the easiest to read of the group, with a median FRE score of 39.6 (‘difficult’).”

## Note

This evidence is mechanism-bounded because it documents readability difficulty rather than directly explaining how file content produces downstream agent behavior.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-002
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: result
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Section 4.1.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.12884v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Agent context files typically use one H1 heading, around 6 to 7 H2 headings, and deeper headings are rare.

## Source Excerpt

“The structure is almost always a single, top-level H1 heading, with a median count of 1.0 for all context files... the context files branch into a moderate number of H2 headings (median 6 to 7)... Deeply nested structures (H5 and beyond) are extremely rare.”

## Note

The source also reports that H5 headers appear in only 6 Claude Code files and H6 headers are non-existent.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-002
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: metric
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Section 4.2.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.12884v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The maintenance analysis covered 5,655 Claude Code commits, 2,767 OpenAI Codex commits, and 2,237 GitHub Copilot commits that added, deleted, or modified context files.

## Source Excerpt

“This yields 5,655 commits for Claude Code, 2,767 commits for OpenAI Codex, and 2,237 commits for GitHub Copilot.”

## Note

These are commits touching context files, not all repository commits.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-002
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: result
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Section 4.2.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.12884v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
A majority of context files were modified in multiple commits: 67.4% for Claude Code, 59.7% for GitHub Copilot, and 59.2% for OpenAI Codex.

## Source Excerpt

“A majority of Claude Code context files (67.4%) are modified in multiple commits... This pattern also holds, though slightly less frequent, for GitHub Copilot (59.7%) and OpenAI Codex (59.2%).”

## Note

The source presents this as evidence that these files are routinely revisited rather than written once.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-002
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: metric
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Section 4.2.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.12884v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Median intervals between context-file updates were 24.1 hours for Claude Code, 22.0 hours for OpenAI Codex, and 70.7 hours for GitHub Copilot.

## Source Excerpt

“Figure 5(b) shows that the median interval is 70.7 hours (around 3 days) for GitHub Copilot, 24.1 hours (around 1 day) for Claude Code, and 22.0 hours (around 1 day) for OpenAI Codex.”

## Note

The source states these interval distributions differ significantly across all compared pairs with p < 0.001.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-002
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: result
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Section 4.2.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.12884v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Context-file evolution is dominated by additions, while deletions remain small; deletions have median values below 15.0 words, and Claude Code commits add a median of 57.0 words.

## Source Excerpt

“Deletions are consistently negligible across all context file types, with median values less than 15.0 words. In contrast, additions are more substantial and variable. Claude Code updates tend to be the most substantial, with a median of 57.0 words added per commit.”

## Note

The source says Claude Code additions exceed OpenAI Codex and GitHub Copilot, although the effect size is negligible.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-002
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: method
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Section 4.3.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.12884v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The manual content analysis produced 16 final instruction labels from 332 Claude Code files and 2,069 final label assignments after disagreement resolution.

## Source Excerpt

“This process resulted in 2,227 total label assignments across the 332 files, with 438 instances of disagreement (80.3% of agreement rate)... This conciliation process results in a total of 2,069 labels.”
“We identified 16 distinct labels...”

## Note

The source also states all three inspectors had 4 to 17 years of programming experience.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-002
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: metric
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Table 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.12884v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The most prevalent instruction categories were Testing (75.0%), Implementation Details (69.9%), Architecture (67.7%), Development Process (63.3%), and Build and Run (62.3%).

## Source Excerpt

“Testing” appears at “75.0”; “Impl. Details” at “69.9”; “Architecture” at “67.7”; “Development Process” at “63.3”; and “Build and Run” at “62.3” in Table 3.

## Note

These prevalence rates are reported for the manually analyzed Claude Code subset.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-002
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: result
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Table 3 and Table 4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.12884v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Non-functional categories were uncommon in the manual analysis, with Security at 14.5%, Performance at 14.5%, and UI/UX at 8.7%; automatic classification reached a micro-average F1-score of 0.79 and performed worse on abstract categories such as AI Integration (0.48 F1) and Project Management (0.42 F1).

## Source Excerpt

“Performance (14.5%), Security (14.5%), and UI/UX (8.7%) appear far less frequently.”
“As shown in Table 4... Micro Average 0.73 precision, 0.86 recall, 0.79 F1.”
“AI Integration” has “0.48” F1 and “Project Management” has “0.42” F1.

## Note

This is contradiction-relevant because the source pairs low coverage of some important topics with weaker automatic recognition on some abstract categories, preserving a visible limitation in both manifest content and classification performance. It is mechanism-bounded because the evidence shows scarcity and detection limits rather than directly establishing how these gaps affect downstream agent behavior.
---

contradiction_relevance: none
---
Agent context files typically use one H1 heading, around 6 to 7 H2 headings, and deeper headings are rare.

## Source Excerpt

“The structure is almost always a single, top-level H1 heading, with a median count of 1.0 for all context files... the context files branch into a moderate number of H2 headings (median 6 to 7)... Deeply nested structures (H5 and beyond) are extremely rare.”

## Note

The source also reports that H5 headers appear in only 6 Claude Code files and H6 headers are non-existent.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-002
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: result
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Table 3 and Table 4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2511.12884v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Non-functional categories were uncommon in the manual analysis, with Security at 14.5%, Performance at 14.5%, and UI/UX at 8.7%; automatic classification reached a micro-average F1-score of 0.79 and performed worse on abstract categories such as AI Integration (0.48 F1) and Project Management (0.42 F1).

## Source Excerpt

“Performance (14.5%), Security (14.5%), and UI/UX (8.7%) appear far less frequently.”  
“As shown in Table 4... Micro Average 0.73 precision, 0.86 recall, 0.79 F1.”  
“AI Integration” has “0.48” F1 and “Project Management” has “0.42” F1.

## Note

This is contradiction-relevant because the source pairs low coverage of some important topics with weaker automatic recognition on some abstract categories, preserving a visible limitation in both manifest content and classification performance. It is mechanism-bounded because the evidence shows scarcity and detection limits rather than directly establishing how these gaps affect downstream agent behavior.

This evidence is mechanism-bounded because it documents readability difficulty rather than directly explaining how file content produces downstream agent behavior.
